mnemonics.title: OS Purpose Mnemonic - SERVE
mnemonics.content:
S - Security: Protects system resources
E - Efficiency: Optimizes resource use
R - Resource Management: Manages CPU, memory, I/O
V - Visualization: Provides GUI/CLI interfaces
E - Execution: Enables program execution

mnemonics.title: OS Functions - PAMD TFB
mnemonics.content:
P - Process Management
A - Application Interface (API)
M - Memory Management
D - Device Management
T - Tool Access (utilities)
F - File Management
B - Booting and Bootstrap

mnemonics.title: OS Structure Types - MGHM
mnemonics.content:
M - Monolithic: All in one kernel
G - Microkernel: Minimal kernel, services in user space
H - Hybrid: Combination of monolithic and microkernel
M - Modular: Dynamic loading of modules

mnemonics.title: Kernel Components - PMSIS
mnemonics.content:
P - Process Scheduler
M - Memory Manager
S - System Call Interface
I - I/O Device Controller
S - File System Manager

acronyms.usage: The core software that manages hardware and provides services to applications
Interface through which applications interact with OS services
Visual interface with icons, windows that users interact with
Text-based interface where users type commands
Firmware that initializes hardware during boot process
Standard API for compatibility across Unix-like systems

visualTips.visualization: Picture OS as a 'Bridge' between hardware (computer parts) and software (apps). The bridge connects two sides - hardware on one side, user applications on the other, allowing them to communicate.
Imagine a wedding cake or a pyramid. Bottom layer = Hardware, Middle layers = Device Drivers & Kernel, Top layer = User Interface & Applications. Each layer talks only to adjacent layers.

Think of the Kernel as the 'Brain' or 'Heart' of the OS. It runs continuously in privileged mode, making decisions and controlling everything. Draw a circle in the center representing kernel, surrounded by system services.
Imagine a security checkpoint or passport control. User applications have 'general access' cards, kernel has 'super admin' access. Apps must request kernel to do privileged tasks.
Draw a 'reception desk' at the entrance between user space and kernel space. Applications go to reception (system call interface) to request services like file access or memory allocation.

analogies.analogy: OS is like a 'Government' of a country. It manages resources (roads, electricity, money), provides services (security, education), establishes rules (laws/policies), and ensures different groups (citizens/businesses) can coexist peacefully.

OS is like a 'Restaurant Manager'. The kitchen hardware (CPU, memory) serves customers (applications). The manager allocates tables (memory), assigns chefs (CPU time), handles complaints (errors), and ensures smooth service.

Kernel is like the 'Captain of a Ship'. The captain has ultimate control, makes critical decisions, monitors all systems, and handles emergencies. Other crew members (system services) report to the captain.

Think of a 'One-Stop Government Office' where all departments (file management, memory, process, device drivers) work in one big building. Fast communication but if one department crashes, the whole building may be affected.

Think of a 'Modular Office Building' with separate small rooms for each department. Only essential staff (minimal kernel) works in the main room; other services run in separate rooms (user space). More stable but slower due to message passing.

Boot process is like 'Waking Up' a city in the morning. First, the alarm (BIOS/UEFI) goes off, checks all systems (POST), then wakes up the main controller (bootloader), which starts the daily operations (OS kernel loading).
